name: Backtracking
summary: Build a combination one candidate at a time; when the remaining target hits zero record it, when it goes negative prune, then undo the last choice and try the next.
visualizationIntent: init: show candidates as an array and an empty path stack. loop: pointer i marks the candidate being tried; candidates before start dim so combinations are not double-counted. update: choosing a candidate pushes it onto the path stack and lowers the remaining target; undoing pops it back off. check: when remaining hits zero the whole path flashes green and is recorded into result; when remaining goes negative the branch is pruned. return: the result panel holds every combination that sums to the target. The call-stack panel mirrors the recursion depth.

lineExplanations.3: Create the empty result list that will collect every valid combination.
lineExplanations.4: Create the empty path list — the combination we are currently building.
lineExplanations.5: Start the recursive search from the first candidate with the full target remaining.
lineExplanations.6: Return every combination that was found.
lineExplanations.9: If nothing is left to reach, the current path sums exactly to the target.
lineExplanations.10: Record a snapshot copy of the current path as a valid combination.
lineExplanations.11: Stop this branch — a complete combination was found.
lineExplanations.12: If the running total overshot the target, this branch can never work.
lineExplanations.14: Try each candidate from start onward — starting at start avoids duplicate combinations.
lineExplanations.15: Choose candidate i by pushing it onto the current path.
lineExplanations.16: Recurse with the smaller remaining target, allowing candidate i to be reused.
lineExplanations.17: Undo the choice: pop candidate i off the path and try the next candidate.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: defines the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def combinationSum(self, candidates, target): the entry method receiving the numbers and target.
syntaxExplanations.4: path = [] creates an empty list used like a stack.
syntaxExplanations.5: self.backtrack(...) calls the recursive helper method.
syntaxExplanations.6: return result hands back the list of combinations.
syntaxExplanations.7: A blank line separates the two methods.
syntaxExplanations.8: def backtrack(self, candidates, remaining, start, path, result): the recursive helper.
syntaxExplanations.9: if remaining == 0: is true when the path sums exactly to the target.
syntaxExplanations.10: result.append(list(path)) stores a copy of the path; list(path) makes a snapshot.
syntaxExplanations.12: if remaining < 0: is true when the running sum passed the target.
syntaxExplanations.13: return ends this recursive call for a pruned branch.
syntaxExplanations.14: for i in range(start, len(candidates)): loops i over candidate indices from start.
syntaxExplanations.15: path.append(candidates[i]) pushes the chosen candidate onto the path.
syntaxExplanations.16: self.backtrack(..., remaining - candidates[i], i, ...) recurses; passing i lets the candidate repeat.
syntaxExplanations.17: path.pop() removes the last chosen candidate to backtrack.
